Fern PorlrrcAr PnacrrcEs CounalssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
luly 3,2A03
Derek Johnson, General Manager
Isla Vista Recreation & Park Diskict
967 Embarcadero Del Mar
Isla Vista, CA93ll7
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our tr'ile No. A-03-062
Dear Mr. Johnson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of IsIa Vista park
Director Harley Augustino regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the political
Reform Act (the 'iA'ct').' Please note that the Commission is unable to provide advice on
issues regarding past conduct. (see section s3l la@); regulation 1g32g(bxgxA)&(c).)
QUESTTONS
1. May Director Augustino, an employee of Service Employees International
Union 620 ("SEru 620') as well as an employee of the Coalition foi aLiingWage
("Coalition'), participate in the deliberations and vote on:
a) A contract between the SEIU 620 andthe Isla Vista Recreation & park
District ("park District") affecting the park Diskict,s employees?
b) A Living wage ordinance that is before the park District?
CONCLUSIONS
la) Director Augustino may not make, participate in making, or use his official
position to influence the contract between the SEIU eZO *athe parl District since the
contract would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the union.
Income received by Director Augustino from the Coalition may also be an independent
basis for disqualification from this decision if the decision will have a reasonabiy
foreseeable material financial effect on the Coalition.
' Governm"rt Code sections 81000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appeffat Title 2, sections
78109-18997, ofthe California Code of Regulations.
'€=


Isla Vista Recreation and Park District Director Harley Augustino is a part-time
employee of SEIU 620,wlnchrepresents the employees ofthlparl oistrict. On May 2g,
2003, in a telephone conversation, you noted that SEIU 62O isa nonprofit organization.
Director Augustino is an organizer with SEIU 62Q whose duties inciude assisting the
union with organizingthe temporary employees of the City of Santa Barbara as weII as
some political work regmding state legislation to benefit temporary city employees.
Director Augustino asks whether a contract between the park District and SEIU
620 woild be void because of Government code 1090 considerations as a result ofhis
employment by the union.2 Also, he asks whetherhis participation in the deliberations
and vote on a contract between SEru 620 andthe ParliDistrict would violate the Act,s
conflict-of-interest laws. Each covered employee of the Park District pays monthly dues
to SEIU 620. The amount is a direct function of the amount ofwages-paid to the 
-
employee. Consequently, an increase in wages in the union contract will directly
increase the monthly paym.ents to SEIU 620.
Additionally, Director Augustino is an employee of the Coalition for a Living
Wage (the "Coalition"), a nonprofit organizatron. DirectorAugustino functions as the
chief administrative officer of the Coalition and his job title is Fi"ta Org*izer. His
duties for the Coalition include recruiting and supervising volunteers, sipenrising paid
staff meeting regularly with the board of directors , "arryogout 
programs and directives
from them andperforming other functions as directed Uytfre board. The Coalition is not
operating any programs in Isla Vista at this time, but has done so within the past two
years. Director Augustino questions whether his participation in park District
deliberations and vote on a living wage ordinance for the Park District and certain of its
contractors would be a violation of the conflict-of-interest laws.
File No. A-03-062
Page No.2
lb) Director Augustino may not make, partic$atein making, oi use his official
position to influence the living wa1e ordinance if a nCxus exists befieen his public duties
and his role as an organizer for the union. Income received by Director Augustino from
the Coalition may also be an independent basis for disqualification from thii decision if
the decision will have areasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Coalition,
as discussed below-
tr'ACTS
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit apublic official from ,.making,,,
"participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to ..influence,, a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial interlst. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
2 Our advice is limited to provisions of the Political Reform Act. Director Augustino may also
want to request advice from the Offrce of the Attorney General about how provisions oicor"*111"nt code
section 1090 may affect his situation.


File No. A-03-062
PageNo.3
officialhas a disqu alifuingconflict of interest (regulation lST11,subdivisions (bxl) -
(8)), which is discussed below. The general rule, ho*ru"r, is that a conflict of interest
may occur whenever a public official makes a governmental decision which has a
reasonably foreseeabl e material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial
interests.
SteDs One and Two: Is Director Augusfino a 6srrublic oflicialr and i, h" g-"kirrOr
*participating in making'or "influencing, a governmental decisio,n?
As a director on the Isla Vista Recreation & Park DistrictBoard ofDirectors,
Director Augustino is a public official as dessribed in section 82048. (SavareeAdvice
Letter, No- 4-02-268-) Your inquiry presupposes that he will at least jarticipate in
making governmental decisions relative to the Park District employeeis contract and the
living w age ordinance. 3
step Three: rMhat are Director Augustinors economic interests?
The next portion of the conflict-of-interest analysis is identification of the public
official's economic interest whictr, under section r7l1a,maybe any of the following:
o 
d public official has an economicinterest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investmenta of $2,000 ormore (section 87103(a);
regulation I 87 03.1 (a));
' 
A public official has an economic interest in anybusiness entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, tntetee, empliyee, or hords aiy position of
management (section 87103(d); regulation lS703.l(b)); 
' ' 
;
' 
A public official has an economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has
a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section SZiO:6Uy; regulation
78703.2);
o 
{ public official has an economic interest in any source of incomel, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior Io the
decision (section 37 1 03 (c); regulation Ig7 03.3);
3 Regulations 18702.1through 187023 defin-e *making," "participating in making,,, and ,.using or
attempting to use hislher official position to influence" the making of gorr**"rrtal decisi6ns. Regulation
18700(b) describes in detail the step-by-step process used to determini if a public official has a conflict of
interest in a particular decision.
a An "indir""t investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or
dependent child ofapublic official, by an agent on behalfofapublic of{icial, or by a busin"ss entity or
trust in which the of{icial, the official's agents,_spouse, and dependent children own directly,indirectly, or
beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater.,, (Section g7l03.i
' The definition of "income" includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity in
which the official owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (section azo:o1a;.;


I'ile No. A-03-062
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in any source of Sifts to him orher if
the gifts aggtegate to $340 or more within 12 months prior io the decision (section
87 1 03 (e); regulation 187 03.4);
r 
d public official has an economic interest in his or herpersonal exp-enses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate familya - this is the
"p ers onal financi al effe cts', rule (section g7 fi3 ; regulation I g7 $:r.
Both SEIU 620 and the Coalition are economic interests to DirectorAugustino,
becausg we presume, both entities have been a source of $500 or more in income to him
over the past twelve months.T These are the only economic interests evident from your
account of the facts, and our analysis will be limited to conflicts of interest growing out
of these economic interests-
SteP Four: Are Director Augustino's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in a governmental deeision?
The next step is to detErmine whether the official's economic interests will be
involved directly or indirectly in a decision. @egulation 19700(b)(4).) A person,
including a business entity or source of incomg is directly involved in-a decision before
an ofEcial's agency whe,n that person, either directry or by an agent:
*(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, orsimilar
request or;
*Q) Is a named party in, or is the zubject o{, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official,s
agency. A person is the zubject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuancg renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person." @egulation rg704.1(a).)
SEru 620 is directly involved in the decision regarding the contract since the
union is a party to the contract, which makes regulation 1870i.3(a) the applicable
standard. (Regulatio n 187 04.1 (a)Q), Humbert Advice Letter, No. I-9 1 -522.)
As to the living wageordinance decision, neither SEIU 620 northe Coalition is a
named patty or subject of the proceeding and is not initiating a proceeding. Regulation
18704-1(b) states that if a source of income is not directly involved in a gJvernmental
decision, then it is determined to be indirectly involved and regulation tSZOS.:6U;
a
6 "Immediate family" includes the official's spous,e and dependent children. (Section g2;2g.)
7 Note that payments from a source are aggregated over the t z months prior to a decision to
determine if the source is a potentially disqualifuing economic interest. Thus, in a case where penodic
payments are made, the total received should be recalculated as earlierpa5rments become more than 12
months old.


materiality standard?
Different standards apply to deterrnine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on therruto* ofthe economic
interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the agency,s
decision.
Since SEIU 62O is directly invgl,ved in a governmental docision (the contract
negotiations), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect ofthat decision upon SEIU 620,
even one penny, ispresumed to bematerial. (Regulation 18705.3(a).) Thereforq if there'
is any.rexorrably foreseeable financial effect on Sfru 62A,Direcior eugustino may not
particrpate in decisions unless an exception applies. underregulation tdloa,an effect
upon economic interests is considered reasonably foreseeableif tt o" is a zubstantial
likelihood that it will occur. A financial effect nled notbe certain to be considered
reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possib ility. (In re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC ops. 19s.) In determining whetho u goi"-*ental decision will have a
reasonably foreseeablematerial financial ef,fect oo *1*rro.ic interest, an official may
consider, among other relevant facts, the factors listed in regulation l g7il16r.
You haveprovided facts indicating that there would be an ef,[ect on SEIU 620,s
income from membership dues if the ernployee salaries were to rise. This is because you
stated that there is direct relationship between the amount of the ernployee salaries and
the amount of the dues paid. Therefore, a conkact increasing or decreasing salaries
would affect the dues paid to SEru 620 andwould be a reasonably forese"-uUt" material
financial effect.
File No. A-$-A62
Page No. 5
applies. Thereforg SEIU 620 andthe Coaliti on areindirectly involved in the ordinance
decision and the coalition is also indirectly in the contract negotiations.
Since the Coalition and SEIU 620 arenon-profit organizations indirectly involved
in the decision on the living wage ordinance 6as wilt as the indirect involvement of the
Coalition in the contract negotiations), the materiality standard of regulation
18705'3(b)(2) would be applicable. Regulation 18705.3(bX2) (cop/enclos"d; list, the
thresholds to test whether the effect of the decision is considered a material effect on a
nonprofit organization. Because "materiality,, depends on the size of the rorpront
organization based on gross annual receipts, which we do not know, we haveLclosed
the regulation for Director Augustino to apply to determine if the reasonablyforeseeable
effect of either decision would be material.


File No. A_03-062
Page No. 6
According to the facts provided, any effect on the Coalition does not appear to
Iikely- The Coalition does not appear to be finan cially irnpacted based on the results of
either decision- If that is trug then the Coalition is not cause of a conflict of interest for
Director Augustino. If there is an impact on the Coalition because of either the contract
negotiations or the living wage ordinancg however, anditmeets the appppriate
threshold, then DirectorAugustino is disqualified from that decision
As for SEru 620, it seems reasonably foreseeable, based on the facts provided,
that the living wage ordinance would probably have some effect "" aEru;r0 although it
is unclear if that effect is large enough to meet the guidelines provided in regulation
18705.3(b)Q). Director Augustino should consider this when making his as"sessment as
to whether such a decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon SEIU 620- Whether a govemmental decision will have areasonably foreseeable
matetial financial "ry:t is a factual question that has to be answered decision-by-
decision- As zuch, ulfimately it is for thepublic ofificial, and not for us, to ans#ers But,
if so, then Director Augustino is disqualified from anyparticipation in iheliving wage
ordinance decision-
Nexus
Also, there is a separate and distinctmateialitystandard which applies in cases
where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to u rorri." of income and to the official,s
public agency. Regulation I 8705.3(c) provides:
"(c) Nexus- Any reasonabry foreseeabre financiar effect on
aperson who is a source of income to apublic official is
.deemed material if thepublic official reCeives or is
promised the income to achieve a goal orpuq)ose which
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the
decision."
Both of these materiality standards (indirectly involved nonprofits and the nexus
standards) would be applicable when a decision .ouid have aforeseeable financial effect
on either the Coalition or SEru 620 if thenexus test is met.e The o"fy aid*""
between these mateiality standards, in this case, is the size of the financial effect that
constitutes a"mateial" effect. Under regulation 18705.3(c) , any ftnanciatteffect is
"mateial." Thus, if the reasonably foreseeable effect of agovemmental decisi onis any
financial gain or loss to the union, that gain or loss is , matJrial,, and we need not
separately consider whether the gain or loss reaches the threshold amounts of regulation
18705.3(b)(2) - the indirect standard.
8 The commission does not act as a finder of fact when it offers advice or assistance. (Regulation
18329(b)(7); In re Ogles by (197 5) 1 FppC Ops. 7 1, 77 .)
'The nexus test could be met if the Coalition is not only a source of income to Director
Augustino, but also his job duties include advocating programs ielated to economic justice including a
"living wage" for the coalition, however, since there ipp"u.r to be no reasonably foieseeable financial
effect on the coalition for either decision, nexus will noi be examined further.


File No. A-03-062
Page No. 7
For the nexus test to be met for SEIU 62A,DirectorAugustino must not only
receive income from the union, but his job duties must include attempting to increase the
salaries of the workers of Isla Vista. An overlap ofjob duties would establish a "nexus,,
between his public and private obligations. From the facts provided, his job title is
"orgarrizef'and his job duties include assisting theunion with organizingtheternporary
employees of the City of Santa Barbara as well as somepolitical work regarding state
legislation to benefit temporary city employees.
There will only be a "nexus" between DirectorAugustino's private-sector
obligations and decisions relating to the living wage ordinance or the contract
negotiations if it is reasonablyforeseeable that the yote on the ordinance or the contract
by the Park District would aid or hinder the accomplishment of his job duties with SEru
620. T?lLe existence of a disqualifuing nexus therefore twns on whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that DirectorAugustino would achieye a goal orpurpose ofhis
ernployment if the living wage ordinance is passed, or lose that opportunity if the
ordinance fails. Or, if the contract negotiations benefit or disfavor one side or the other
thereby achieving a goal orpuq)ose ofhis employment.
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeablg
within the meaning of section 87703, ifit is "substantially likely''that one ormore of the
materiality standards aprylicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of the
govemmental decision. @egulation 18706; In re Thorner, supra.) A financial effect
need not be a certatnty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is zol reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner, supra.)
Whether the financial consequences.are reasonably foreseeable at the time of a
govemmental decision depends on the facts of each particular case. Qd.) In each casg,
the official must look at the facts surrounding each decision to determine whether the
decision is substantially likely to cause a material financial effect on the economic
interest. (/d.)
In the present case, it is reasonably foreseeable that either a vote on the living
wage ordinance or negotiations for a new labor contract would aid or hinder his organizer
duties for SEIU 620, so a nexus would exist and any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on the SEru 620 would cause Director Augustino to be disqualified from that
decision. Since we have determined earlier in this analysis that any decision that affects
the wages of union employees will have an effect on SEIU 620,Director Augustino is
disqualified from participating in those types of decisions including the contract
negotiations and, possibly, the living wage ordinance.
"Public
,,
Exceptions
We have not gone on to discuss the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on an official,s
economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generalTy.
(Regulation 18707.) Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally


File No. A-03-062
Page No. 8
required to participate in the decision, Nothing in your account of the facts indicates that
either of these exceptions would apply to DirectorAugustino, but we call your attention
to them in case facts arise where one or the other excqrtion might apply.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Galena West
Counsel, Legal Division

18705'3(b)(2) would be applicable. Regulation 18705.3(bX2) (cop/enclos"d; list, the
thresholds to test whether the effect of the decision is considered a material effect on a
nonprofit organization. Because "materiality,, depends on the size of the rorpront
organization based on gross annual receipts, which we do not know, we haveLclosed
the regulation for Director Augustino to apply to determine if the reasonablyforeseeable
effect of either decision would be material.


File No. A_03-062
According to the facts provided, any effect on the Coalition does not appear to
Iikely- The Coalition does not appear to be finan cially irnpacted based on the results of
either decision- If that is trug then the Coalition is not cause of a conflict of interest for
Director Augustino. If there is an impact on the Coalition because of either the contract
negotiations or the living wage ordinancg however, anditmeets the appppriate
threshold, then DirectorAugustino is disqualified from that decision
As for SEru 620, it seems reasonably foreseeable, based on the facts provided,
that the living wage ordinance would probably have some effect "" aEru;r0 although it
is unclear if that effect is large enough to meet the guidelines provided in regulation
18705.3(b)Q). Director Augustino should consider this when making his as"sessment as
to whether such a decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
upon SEIU 620- Whether a govemmental decision will have areasonably foreseeable
matetial financial "ry:t is a factual question that has to be answered decision-by-
decision- As zuch, ulfimately it is for thepublic ofificial, and not for us, to ans#ers But,
if so, then Director Augustino is disqualified from anyparticipation in iheliving wage
ordinance decision-
Nexus
Also, there is a separate and distinctmateialitystandard which applies in cases
where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to u rorri." of income and to the official,s
public agency. Regulation I 8705.3(c) provides:
"(c) Nexus- Any reasonabry foreseeabre financiar effect on
aperson who is a source of income to apublic official is
.deemed material if thepublic official reCeives or is
promised the income to achieve a goal orpuq)ose which
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the
decision."
Both of these materiality standards (indirectly involved nonprofits and the nexus
standards) would be applicable when a decision .ouid have aforeseeable financial effect
on either the Coalition or SEru 620 if thenexus test is met.e The o"fy aid*""
between these mateiality standards, in this case, is the size of the financial effect that
constitutes a"mateial" effect. Under regulation 18705.3(c) , any ftnanciatteffect is
"mateial." Thus, if the reasonably foreseeable effect of agovemmental decisi onis any
financial gain or loss to the union, that gain or loss is , matJrial,, and we need not
separately consider whether the gain or loss reaches the threshold amounts of regulation
18705.3(b)(2) - the indirect standard.
8 The commission does not act as a finder of fact when it offers advice or assistance. (Regulation
18329(b)(7); In re Ogles by (197 5) 1 FppC Ops. 7 1, 77 .)
'The nexus test could be met if the Coalition is not only a source of income to Director
Augustino, but also his job duties include advocating programs ielated to economic justice including a
"living wage" for the coalition, however, since there ipp"u.r to be no reasonably foieseeable financial
effect on the coalition for either decision, nexus will noi be examined further.
For the nexus test to be met for SEIU 62A,DirectorAugustino must not only
receive income from the union, but his job duties must include attempting to increase the
salaries of the workers of Isla Vista. An overlap ofjob duties would establish a "nexus,,
between his public and private obligations. From the facts provided, his job title is
"orgarrizef'and his job duties include assisting theunion with organizingtheternporary
employees of the City of Santa Barbara as well as somepolitical work regarding state
legislation to benefit temporary city employees.
There will only be a "nexus" between DirectorAugustino's private-sector
obligations and decisions relating to the living wage ordinance or the contract
negotiations if it is reasonablyforeseeable that the yote on the ordinance or the contract
by the Park District would aid or hinder the accomplishment of his job duties with SEru
620. T?lLe existence of a disqualifuing nexus therefore twns on whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that DirectorAugustino would achieye a goal orpurpose ofhis
ernployment if the living wage ordinance is passed, or lose that opportunity if the
ordinance fails. Or, if the contract negotiations benefit or disfavor one side or the other
thereby achieving a goal orpuq)ose ofhis employment.
A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeablg
within the meaning of section 87703, ifit is "substantially likely''that one ormore of the
materiality standards aprylicable to that economic interest will be met as a result of the
govemmental decision. @egulation 18706; In re Thorner, supra.) A financial effect
need not be a certatnty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand, if an
effect is only a mere possibility, it is zol reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner, supra.)
Whether the financial consequences.are reasonably foreseeable at the time of a
govemmental decision depends on the facts of each particular case. Qd.) In each casg,
the official must look at the facts surrounding each decision to determine whether the
decision is substantially likely to cause a material financial effect on the economic
interest. (/d.)
In the present case, it is reasonably foreseeable that either a vote on the living
wage ordinance or negotiations for a new labor contract would aid or hinder his organizer
duties for SEIU 620, so a nexus would exist and any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on the SEru 620 would cause Director Augustino to be disqualified from that
decision. Since we have determined earlier in this analysis that any decision that affects
the wages of union employees will have an effect on SEIU 620,Director Augustino is
disqualified from participating in those types of decisions including the contract
negotiations and, possibly, the living wage ordinance.
"Public
,,
Exceptions
We have not gone on to discuss the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on an official,s
economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generalTy.
(Regulation 18707.) Step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally
required to participate in the decision, Nothing in your account of the facts indicates that
either of these exceptions would apply to DirectorAugustino, but we call your attention
to them in case facts arise where one or the other excqrtion might apply.

foreseeable material financial effect on the Coalition.
